questions.question: What is the fundamental purpose of a Certificate Authority (CA) in the Web PKI?
options: To encrypt all traffic between a browser and a web server
To generate private keys for website owners
To verify domain/organization identity and sign a binding between that identity and a public key
To maintain a global directory of every TLS connection
questions.explanation: A CA acts as a trusted third party that validates the certificate applicant's control over a domain (or verifies their legal identity for EV certs) and digitally signs an X.509 certificate that binds a public key to that identity. Browsers trust this binding by verifying the CA's signature against pre-installed root certificates.

questions.question: How does SSH's trust model differ from the Web PKI?
options: SSH does not use public key cryptography at all
SSH uses Trust On First Use (TOFU) — the host key is recorded on first connection and checked on subsequent connections
SSH uses the same CA infrastructure as HTTPS
SSH requires a third-party certificate for every host
questions.explanation: SSH uses TOFU: the first time you connect to a server, its host key is saved to ~/.ssh/known_hosts. If the key changes later, SSH warns you. This avoids the complexity of a global PKI but is vulnerable on the very first connection (no prior trust anchor). In contrast, Web PKI relies on pre-installed root CA certificates as trust anchors.

questions.question: What problem does Certificate Transparency solve that CRLs and OCSP do not?
options: Certificate Transparency reduces the size of certificates
Certificate Transparency provides a publicly auditable, append-only log of all issued certificates, making it possible to detect mis-issuance quickly
Certificate Transparency replaces OCSP for real-time revocation checking
Certificate Transparency encrypts certificate contents to prevent eavesdropping
questions.explanation: CRLs and OCSP only tell you whether a certificate has been revoked after the fact — but if a CA is compromised or mis-issues a certificate, you may never know to check for revocation. CT makes every certificate issuance publicly visible in an append-only Merkle tree. Anyone can monitor CT logs and detect an unauthorized certificate for their domain within hours — as opposed to the months or years it took to discover the DigiNotar breach.

questions.question: Why is the Subject Alternative Name (SAN) extension critical for modern TLS certificate validation?
options: The SAN provides the certificate's serial number for revocation checks
The SAN lists the DNS names and IP addresses for which the certificate is valid, and modern browsers exclusively check SANs (ignoring the Common Name field)
The SAN contains the CA's signature for chain validation
The SAN specifies which cryptographic algorithms the certificate supports
questions.explanation: The SAN extension (OID 2.5.29.17) lists all domains, IP addresses, and other identifiers for which the certificate is authorized. Since RFC 6125 and modern browser policies (Chrome 58+, Safari, Firefox), the Common Name (CN) field is no longer checked for hostname matching — only SANs are evaluated. A certificate without the correct SAN for a domain will trigger a certificate name mismatch error, even if the CN is correct.

questions.question: What problem does Certificate Transparency (CT) solve?
options: Making certificates cheaper to obtain
Detecting misissued or fraudulent certificates by requiring all certificates to be logged in public, append-only logs
Encrypting certificate contents
Automatically renewing expiring certificates
questions.explanation: Before CT, a compromised CA could issue fraudulent certificates silently. CT requires all issued certificates to be logged in public, cryptographically verifiable logs. Browsers can check logs to ensure a certificate was legitimately issued, and domain owners can monitor for unauthorized certificates.
